item.title: Current Songs on Repeat
item.description:
Track Artist
Untitled God Song Haley Heynderickx
Let Down Radiohead
the cure Olivia Rodrigo
maggots for brains Olivia Rodrigo
Everybody Scream Flroence + the Machine
i saw you in a dream The Japanese House
Sweet Nothing Calvin Harris
Sienna The Marias
No matching items

item.title: April Showers
item.dc:creator: Marlin Alejandro
item.description:
The saying goes that April showers brings May flowers. The penultimate month for me; four years really pass by.

I initially entered my spring semester with a feeling of fulfillment but also dread. Dread for what comes next, for what might be lost, dread of closing this chapter of my life. I still remember hearing my older sister talk about Wellesley College. I was captivated by this small liberal arts college and I made it my dream to apply there. Two years of convincing and proving that I can be independent to my parents, two years of meticulously planning the financial situations that will come, calculating the aid and coverage. Two years of crying over arguments with my parents as we discussed the idea of me leaving. No one has ever studied out-of-state; not in my family, or anyone we knew. In our culture, that is not something that exists. You live with your family, until you marry, and sometimes, even married you still live with them—and how could I choose to leave. Because that is what I was doing. I was being selfish in choosing to leave, knowing my sisters didn’t have the opportunity to, knowing a million family concerns would not be on my shoulders, knowing that I was leaving a heavier burden on my family.

                   "Show me the heart unfettered by foolish dreams, and I'll show you a happy man."
       
  "But only in their dreams can men truly be free"

But, I wanted to be free in my dreams of Wellesley. I had already envisioned myself in her embrace, how could I refuse this call? I feared I would resent my family if I didn’t go. So I left—

I have come to learn that Wellesley while my dream, like any dream goes, can be good or bad. Nightmares are dreams too, and in all the love I have for Wellesley, there were times where I wished I was home, where I felt confined in the four walls we call dorm and wondered if I truly belonged. Even with my friends, I felt lonely at times. Humans at their core, desire and need human connection. I was making connections at Wellesley, but the connections I craved the most were at home, in New York. But my life at Wellesley, like all things—passes.

April

This April has been what I call my month of contemplation. My month of magical thinking. Maybe it was because I was reaching my end (college end that is), and after four years I have learned the in’s and out’s of Wellesley; her secrets and my fears and dreams. In her embrace I learned to deconstruct myself and accept the jagged pieces. ‘Identity’, that was what my pediatric psychiatrist had said I struggled with. The struggles of 12 year old me continue to haunt me. It sounds more daunting and horrifying than what it was, or maybe it was that horrifying; the process itself was painful, I tore at myself but also stitched myself anew—and how wonderful it was afterwards. Like my skin was too tight for who I was, and now under the new stitches I had made, I could finally breathe. But with this newer skin of mine, I still was met with hurdles.

My head hurt everyday for the past two years, I thought myself the woman in The Yellow Wallpaper, and thought I would go mad, images of my head tearing open like the birth of Athena from Zeus’s head. And just like the Zeus, I too birthed something from my mind: a gentler version of myself. With pain, with my pain, I learned to care for myself in ways I couldn’t bother to before. Who knew pain could create such a thing.

This new version of myself was gentler, but not to others, only to me. She was made for me, and that was all the more special. She visited me in the quietest nights, when I would massage at my frail body, praying that whatever I did in the past to deserve this pain, was worth it. I figured that maybe I committed treason against a monarch—or I caused a war. I must have been very evil and cruel to be suffering. This was the only thing I could think of, otherwise, I would be distraught on the implications that my pain has no reason behind it.

Maybe it is all just a conjuration of the mind, any doctor visit further cemented the idea. I must have made up everything, but for what? I’m still trying to figure that out. But, even with all this pain and suffering that Wellesley has seen me experience/caused, she has also nurtured the wild animal in me that bites. A dog that bites is not a good dog, but why does a dog have to be good? Under whose eyes? God? Human? In her nature, I found peace and fulfillment, I truly think I was meant to lie on the ground and be overtaken by the moss. In her lustrous greens, I found joy and my bones always felt lighter.

Besides being my month of contemplation, it was also my month of isolation. Illness, besides my frequent migraines racked my body continuously, and I became even more familiar with the shadows of my room. I have become so familiar with the way the sun rose and shined on my bookshelf that I could tell what time it was simply by the way the beams of sun hit the wooden frame. I wanted to go home, being sick and alone is most depressing. I would look at my calendar—only a few more weeks—I’ll be home soon.

I have decided to forgo any negative thoughts and instead share the Wellesley that I love with my friends. I went on multiple car trips with no permanent destination in mind, only talk and music. I convinced one of my dearest friends (very little convincing on my end) to walk with me (after I had a breakdown as one does). They showed me the little bridge by the old Paintshop and we carried seeds in our palms, watching the birds flying by.

item.title: book runs and hospital lights
item.dc:creator: Marlin Alejandro
item.description:
My mother is feeling ill—she has been sick her whole life, a sickness that exhausts the body. It’s genetic—this disease…but that is not what we are here for. The hospital lights are always blinding, the shuffling of staff and patients, the dull hum of the central unit—the ache building in my shoulders—I always carry my stress there.

My head hurts again, I’ve been here for over 9 hours. the stress will kill me, I’ve started to become numb again from the pain…I’ve become a sort of hospital ghost–floating around the hallways, standing still at times–the rain continued outside.

it will pass    eventually

item.title: May Flowers
item.dc:creator: Marlin Alejandro
item.description:
Here are the may flowers, and there are plenty on campus.

The days are approaching, the memories fleeting, like the cherry blossoms on campus. My college life seems to be slipping from my fingers and with it the end of my teenage years and the beginning of my 20s.

I often feel as if I missed out on milestones that people my age are expected to reach, but then again, I am not everyone, am I?

In my Ecopoetics class, we are required to have a journal: nature journal. Meant for our thoughts as we contemplate and consider our relationship with nature and where we stand. I have taken a liking to going on walks around campus after class. The best thing about this spring semester is the lack of restraints on my schedule. I have actually not written much in my journal, not from lack of thinking, but rather because I tend to carry my own personal journal.

“You probably think that your mom’s meatballs are the best meatballs, just like I think my mom’s meatballs are the best ones, but it could be that neither one makes the best meatballs”

If I am considered to be the best in someone’s eyes, but someone else thinks that another person is the best, am I truly the best? Are we all someone’s best/favorite only to realize that it means that none of us are the best.

Graduation

Graduation came and there was nothing much to say. I wanted to somehow leave a part of me at Wellesley College, so that she would remember me and to prove that I was there. How to leave behind my print?

The graduation passed by quickly– I was sitting one moment for rehearsal and then I was walking on stage, waving at a sea of people whose faces I could not see. I graduated, did you see?

妹妹,这仍在进行中

item.title: thought daughter
item.dc:creator: Marlin Alejandro
item.description:
Fragments of Thoughts that Once Were


Feb 1, 2026


Walked with a friend to buy groceries, we both stood under the beams of the moon. I mentioned my favorite story, one that my father used to tell me:

 A man who tended his land all day,

Never resting, never resting, always busy —

The Moon approached him one night, begged him to go home,

But there can be no home without labour. 

As punishment, for ignoring her pleas,

  he was made to tend his land eternally, 

His wife and child, figments of the past.


 She lent me her ear, eyes searching for the farmer and his ox. Is he still tending to his land? I shrugged, my fingers numb from the cold, I like to think the Moon granted him peace eventually, she is after all a guardian. We stood in silence, no movement from either of us; Our breaths were almost still that we could have passed as ghosts. I smiled brightly at her, holding out my hand. It’s cold, let’s go inside. We ran toward the dim light of the store, laughing, our forms flickering like two stars in the sky.

“I see the moon, and the moon sees somebody I want to see”

Feb 1, 2026


Shadows are essentially a proof of existence.  Even while not looking directly at the tree, its shadow must mean that it exists; it moves, as if it were its own separate entity. It would be interesting to think of when there are no shadows. When light hits an object or a living being in a certain way, no shadow is produced. Would it really exist? If we could not see it, and there was no shadow, can we say something is real? 

Feb 4, 2026


I sit beside my friend, 

rays of sun bouncing against the wooden table. 

Miniscule dust particles shimmering under the light, 

peelings of a clementine —

Home.

bowls of peeled and cut fruit,

 Distant chatter coming from the kitchen:

My mother’s voice —

 I am cold.

 I’ll call my mom later

Feb 7, 2026


Snow,

Snow in spring,

Shoes all wet,

Shoes dripping, 

Wet,

Wet,

Cold,

Cold,

I will make tea —


forgetting to check the forecast

Feb 11, 2026


The snow slushed

 under my shoes,

Is spring coming soon? 

I look at my watch, 

Tick   Tick     Tick

running late—

Fear of slipping.

Fear of loss


Shadow of a lamp,

Black on a pristine white canvas, 

Glistening under the sun,

 I want to lie upon it,

 The snow will cushion me. 

I stop.

I stare.

How time flies, 

I’ll leave tomorrow.


Feb 15, 2026


Hop —

   —  Hop —-

—Hop —-     Hop—-


I wonder where the rabbits go in this cold

She looks at me

And I at her,

Two creatures under the same sky.

 I hope she makes it home safe, 

guided by the dim lights of the lamps, 

the moon hidden. 


Feb 18, 2026


I walk across campus,

Stepping –  stepping — 

Fluffy white, crushed under my shoes. 

Mary Janes. 

The front of my shoes, scuffed —

How many years? 

Only yesterday my older sister gifted me these shoes. 

Her first paycheck,

 I lift my foot,

a mark remains. 

It will be erased.

Feb 21, 2026


Waking up at 4 a.m.


The lamposts illuminate the walkpath, 

dark looming pines leaning, 

telling each other secrets. 

The snow weighing heavy on their limbs, 

I stand under such trees,

 Wonder,

 if I too can hear their secrets

 if I stay still for long enough,

 A minute passes,

 or maybe seconds.

 I continue my trek,

 maybe when I am not in a rush,

 I will have the time to listen. 



March 7, 2026


My tulips bloomed, 

Maybe spring is coming afterall. 

The drip-drop of the snow melting at my window. 

It feels weird seeing my tulips bloom,

after everything that happened. 


My tulips white. 

My tulips white as the snow outside. 

White tulips stand for innocence, 

Forgiveness,

new beginnings. 

How strange,

My tulips decided to bloom today. 


“Sad news regarding a member of our campus community” - 7:35 p.m.




March 16, 2026


milky white fog

 hides the tall buildings, 

raindrops on the branches, 

chirping of birds, 

hands are in my pockets, 

i let out a breath, 

watching the fog move,

move like a stream, 

Like soft waves —

Humming,

Hum, 

I wait



March 30, 2026


The trees are still bare —

Small fiery leaves still clinging on,

To a spring that is around the corner, 

But these leaves,

These leaves will not last.


New buds are coming in,

Speckles of green ready to bloom,

And those forgotten leaves,

Valiant leaves that held throughout winter,

They will fall, for spring to come



Mar 30, 2026

Lines Written on a Rainy Walk


A forgotten roll lays on the ground,

Maybe someone was hungry, 

And that hunger must have fled

And taken flight

Because now the roll is covered in soil,

The rain, dusting it over.


I am not the only one

Who passes by the roll,

Eyes glazing over, lips pursed,

Littering—

I however, feel as the roll


Feel the people passing by me, 

Watching, observing, but never stopping

Remaining untouched,

I am untouched,

Untouched –


In the end

The birds and the embrace of a mother

Will cradle my broken begotten body,

Will love only come at death?

Does one have to die,

To feel the warmth?

To a Friend,


It’s almost the end of the semester. Today, the weather is beautiful, the birds are singing again, and the daffodil bulbs look about ready to bloom. I hope that you can feel the beginning of spring, wherever you are now. I went back home…the same place we both called home — did you think of home? Of the warmth that comes when stepping foot in a city that has seen you grow — I went to the mall, the one that we both talked about going during high school, teenagers were everywhere, a reminder of what we once were. The spot that we both call “ our favorite”, still stands. I look away. My throat dry, a huge lump of possibilities being pushed down, hand clenching shirt, clawing at my heart, tear it out, tearing it out.

Eyes burning, heat flaring behind my skull, get out, leave now.

I wish you could have gone back home, you should have been here, standing where I am.

I’m sorry — I hope you found peace. 



April 3, 2026


One turtle,

Two turtles,

Three turtles,

Heads raised up,

Beams of sun

Reflecting on their shells —

I miss home.

April 3, 2026


One geese, 

Two geese, 

Lying on the vibrant green.

Long necks curled in, 

Enjoying the summer breeze.


April 3, 2026


“The bright song of the Tufted Titmouse is one of the first sounds of spring across Eastern North America.”


Warmth dances across my face

Wind blowing gently,

Kissing my nape,

The song of the titmouse

Surrounding me,

I could almost grow wings

And take flight

April 6, 2026


In ancient times, it was believed that flowers were red from the spilled blood of innocents. I wonder how it came to be, that red flowers are gifted for deep love. When the crimson would go deep into mother earth, tainting the once white flowers, did we decide then, that the only way to penance is to take the sacrifice of the innocent and take this bloodied flower as an act of devotion, a symbol of love rather than loss.


April 6, 2026


Humanity shifted the moment we decided to hunt whales. 

Whales, who are one of our eldest ancestors, whose eyes have knowledge 

that we can only dream of learning. 

Whose songs, up close, pull deep, deep inside you,

Who stay with their dead calfs, pushing them for days,

 Mourning —

 When the last Dodo was killed for being kind, 

we doomed our planet. 

When the last Stellar’s cow whose only fault was her own curiosity, 

approaching nearby boats, then,

we must have cursed our future generations. 

When sharks were finned for sport, their hearts hanging outside their jaws, 

fear and desperation the last emotion they felt. 

Their corpse on display —

When the great lonesome tortoise, George, 

who saw the beginning of civilization, had died, we died with him.

When pigeons were taken as instruments in war; 

messengers that took flight, awarded for heroics, 

only to be called pests, did we fail then?

Did we fail when we sent a stray into space, 

no return flight —

Did we advance as a society or did we fail one another? 

Did our mother,    the earth,    weep when she heard,

“We will drill, baby, drill”; 

when her children d e p a r t e d this world, when her lands were burned. 

We go to the stars above, searching for life, searching for meaning, 

While the ground beneath our feet dries up, water acidic,

organisms that have existed since the time of creation, 

now only 1,000, 

                    now only 100, 

                                        now only 10, 

                                                      soon none.


When the whales were hunted, did they cry, 

tears filling up the seas, 

their calves purposely used as bait. 

Did they decide to stay alongside their only child, 

knowing, 

knowing,

knowing 

that their end would come. 


We are at a time where it matters where you stand - Robin Wall Kimmerer

item.title: Prose
item.dc:creator: Marlin Alejandro
item.description:
A short narrative on my childhood. Written originally as a college entrance essay and later edited and changed into a more poetical format for an English class at said college.

Vocabulary:

Curandero - Healer

Abuelita - Grandmother

Abuelito - Grandfather

Ñaña - Sister 

Ruda - Rue

Hierbabuena - Peppermint

Eucalipto - Eucalyptus

Tabaco - Tabacco


La Curandera: a girl playing healer


Curandero is a term used throughout Ecuador to describe a person who is skilled in the art of herbal medicine and “magical” plants. They are often deemed as healers in my community, using plants to cleanse the body of any evil; of sickness. Growing up in such a community, I was no stranger to these practices, practices that were performed in my household. I remember being a young child, wide-eyed, staring as my abuelita would pass an egg over my ñaña’s frail body. La pasada del huevo is supposed to get rid of any malice, especially that of the evil eye, that children are exposed to easily. After you roll the egg, prayers falling from your lips, intent to heal, present, you crack the egg into a cup of water. Based on the forming of the yolk and whites, you can determine the cause of ailment. 


I take an egg, my hands cupped around it, 

My hands cradling it, as if life were to hatch.

I enter the dark room, my eyes fixed on a sleeping form.

Whose form? Who lies on this white bed— a bed with wheels, a bed on wheels. 

The dripping of a bag going through a line; drip — drip — drip—

Hushed prayers being uttered from my lips, lips that should be producing laughter, 

Lips, child-lips, lips that have cried and that now murmur.

I pass the egg over this clothed body—

My abuelito lays here, he sleeps here, in this strange house,

With strange people, and now a strange girl hovers next to him, holding an egg. 


 Besides la pasada del huevo there are many techniques that are used, requiring medicinal plants. Plants like ruda, hierbabuena, eucalipto, and tabaco, are brushed against the body, purifying the soul. Infusing them in teas and baths, ensures your protection. Hanging them in dried batches can grant you wealth and prosperity. You can not be born in Ecuador without becoming one with her animals, with her hills, with her bountiful nature. I used to consider myself a friend of the plants, proudly telling my American friends that I knew secrets: I knew what the birds would sing, what the trees would say in hushed winds, I could feel the rain before the first drop; I was a child of earth; as a friend of nature, I pestered my grandpa with endless questions about herbs, and all the little critters that hid under rocks and the creatures that stalked from the trees. I wanted to know why the world came to be, and why plants provided us medicine. 


    Who are you? 

 Where am I? — 

Was I wrong? My shaky hands clenched behind my red dress,

Red like the color of his car; red like his favorite shirt;

Red like crimson, the crimson that bloomed on the ground.

Eyes downcast, lips pursed.

Why didn’t it work? It should have worked?

Warm the egg. Check. 

Have intent. Check.

Pray. Check.

Why – why – why did nothing change?


I quickly realized that I wouldn’t be able to become a curandera, 
so I had shifted my interest from medicinal herbs and focused 
on flowers and the language surrounding them. Much like how a word 
is a symbol with a prescribed definition, flowers all have different 
meanings and interpretations that can be used to communicate and 
form a message with. The idea of communicating with nature, 
through nature, became my secret code, one that I began to share 
with my abuelito. I would laugh before my abuelito would tell me 
to hush, both of us straining our ears to the trees, 
feet grounded, spine straight; 
one must stand like a tree to become one. 


    Trees can be uprooted,

Their roots exposed, what once was mighty

Now the fallen —

But, is it me who is the fallen?

If a tree falls and no one is around to hear.

Did it fall? Did I fall?

When you fell like an uprooted tree, 

Did you make a sound? Your red leaves veiling you


A brain can bleed, a violent crimson can pour from your skull, who knew a skull could be fragile, who knew— A hundred combinations of plants and yet you pain. How many eggs have been used and yet, you do not remember?  How a person whom you have raised can become a stranger, a strange little girl who cradles an egg. You can’t help but look at this sad girl, wondering why she cries, wondering why she holds onto this egg as if it were a lifeline. Little strange girl, don’t cry.

item.title: red moleskin
item.dc:creator: Marlin Alejandro
item.description:
A voice that rings out 

My aunt singing while sewing

Her little escape

  • My aunt at 2 a.m.


I wish to go back to the days

Where my cousins and I 

Would sing and play, 

Soaking the sun. 


When abuelita would call us

To eat hot soup on an even hotter day.

When we would climb mi abuelito’s red car

And pretend we were driving 


Summers that we would spend together

Vividily engraved in my mind

And yet those times spent are all

Now memories, times that will not return,


People we will never see, 

Voices we will never harem

And moments that we enjoyed as naive and innocent children.

When I feel far from home,

I close my eyes and pretend

Being seven in Ecuador. 


*unedited version*

A narrative: something about you, something personal, and yet as I pick up my pen, I can’t write anything about myself. I fear that the damage is rooted deep, the generational trauma still there, and the inability to express my emotions is one scar I have not been able to heal. 

  • I’m sorry that I failed that class


Jugo de Coco


On a hot summer day

My mom would always buy me jugo de coco

It was my favorite drink as a kid

The cart vendor knew to pass by our 

Street, and everyday without miss

A 50 cent drink would be in my hands

As i got older we frequented Ecuador less

Loved ones were lost, crime too high.

It no longer felt like the home I knew when i was 9


And yet last year at 16, i went and 

My aunt called me down and passed me a dollar

She had signaled the vendor to come and with a smile told me to run

Running outside brought me to when i was 9 and for a minute, my barrio looked familiar. 


Flour Hands


In my childhood I loved

To bake

I would help my grandma when she would

Make desserts

“Con delicadeza, mija” she would tell me

The batter has to be mixed consistently 

Changing rhythm would not allow it to rise

I would nod and not let anyone else mix,

After all too many different hands will

Ruin it

Only now as a college student do I realize

My abuelita
